{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "R1.7",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Do you want a surface-level language edit only, or a substantive revision that may reframe the contribution, adjust claims (e.g., the “no prior work” statement), reorganize sections, and propose specific related work and citations? Please choose: [A] polish-only copy edit; [B] full substantive revision (structure, argument, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty/scholarship).",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Clarifying the thesis, gap, and contribution (disentangling visual vs. linguistic ambiguity in VQA) will strengthen the paper’s conceptual positioning.",
    "R1.2": "Checking the argument from problem framing to method design to evaluation ensures premises and conclusions cohere without internal contradictions.",
    "R1.3": "Verifying that claims (e.g., ablation-driven gains and AUROC improvements) are supported by the reported experiments aligns assertions with available evidence.",
    "R1.4": "Estimating novelty versus nearest prior approaches (confidence-thresholding, MC dropout, ensembles, and any ambiguity-disentangling work) is key to calibrating contribution claims.",
    "R1.5": "Reorganizing sections to improve the narrative arc (motivation → method → ablations → evaluation) can enhance readability and emphasis.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level editing for clarity, concision, and tone is essential to make the draft professional and easy to follow.",
    "R1.7": "Adding and verifying citations for VQA, abstention/uncertainty, attention-entropy, and clarifying-question generation will ensure proper attribution and coverage.",
    "RX.1": "We will explicitly note uncertainties where evidence is limited (e.g., small validation subset, scope of ablations).",
    "RX.2": "We will temper or qualify broad claims (like “no prior work” or “drives most of the gain”) to avoid overstating results.",
    "RX.3": "We will stay within improving the draft’s writing and argumentation without overstepping into redesigning experiments.",
    "RX.4": "We will avoid language that could mislead readers about reliability or generality, especially for abstention in safety-relevant contexts.",
    "RX.5": "We will tie each suggested change to specific locations in the draft to preserve provenance and traceability."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Do you want a surface-level language edit only, or a substantive revision that may reframe the contribution, adjust claims (e.g., the “no prior work” statement), reorganize sections, and propose specific related work and citations? Please choose: [A] polish-only copy edit; [B] full substantive revision (structure, argument, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty/scholarship).

rationale.R1.1: Clarifying the thesis, gap, and contribution (disentangling visual vs. linguistic ambiguity in VQA) will strengthen the paper’s conceptual positioning.
rationale.R1.2: Checking the argument from problem framing to method design to evaluation ensures premises and conclusions cohere without internal contradictions.
rationale.R1.3: Verifying that claims (e.g., ablation-driven gains and AUROC improvements) are supported by the reported experiments aligns assertions with available evidence.
rationale.R1.4: Estimating novelty versus nearest prior approaches (confidence-thresholding, MC dropout, ensembles, and any ambiguity-disentangling work) is key to calibrating contribution claims.
rationale.R1.5: Reorganizing sections to improve the narrative arc (motivation → method → ablations → evaluation) can enhance readability and emphasis.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level editing for clarity, concision, and tone is essential to make the draft professional and easy to follow.
rationale.R1.7: Adding and verifying citations for VQA, abstention/uncertainty, attention-entropy, and clarifying-question generation will ensure proper attribution and coverage.
rationale.RX.1: We will explicitly note uncertainties where evidence is limited (e.g., small validation subset, scope of ablations).
rationale.RX.2: We will temper or qualify broad claims (like “no prior work” or “drives most of the gain”) to avoid overstating results.
rationale.RX.3: We will stay within improving the draft’s writing and argumentation without overstepping into redesigning experiments.
rationale.RX.4: We will avoid language that could mislead readers about reliability or generality, especially for abstention in safety-relevant contexts.
rationale.RX.5: We will tie each suggested change to specific locations in the draft to preserve provenance and traceability.
